title: Repairing a chipped bath enamel surface
subtitle: Fill a small chip in a steel or cast-iron bath with two-part enamel repair paint
excerpt: How to fix a small chip in a bath enamel surface before rust forms in the exposed steel beneath. Covers cleaning the chip, picking the matching paint, the two-part build-up coat, and the cure period before the bath is used.

glossaryTerms.definition: A factory-applied vitreous enamel layer fused onto a steel or cast-iron bath shell. Hard and glossy when intact; cracks and chips through impact to expose the steel beneath.

content.text: A chip in
bath enamel

shows the steel beneath the gloss. Water sits in the chip and the steel rusts. Within a year a small chip becomes a rust ring that spreads under the enamel. The fix takes 20 minutes plus a 24-hour cure.
Step 1, dry the bath
Run the bath empty for a day. Wipe the chip with a clean dry cloth. The repair paint will not bond to a damp surface or a soapy film. Leave the bath empty for the whole job and the cure.
Step 2, clean the chip
Wipe the chip and the surrounding enamel with white spirit on a soft cloth. This removes soap and skin oils that block adhesion. Allow the spirit to dry off, two or three minutes.
Step 3, key the chip and remove any rust

Sand the inside of the chip with 240 grit paper on a small wooden stick. If rust has formed in the steel, sand through it back to bright metal. The surrounding enamel should be left untouched but lightly keyed at the chip edge with a few light strokes. Wipe the dust off with a dry cloth.
Step 4, mix the two-part repair

A bath enamel repair kit has a base and a hardener. Squeeze a small amount of each onto a clean lid or a card. Mix to an even colour. The mixed paint has a working time of about 20 minutes before it tacks.
Step 5, build up the chip in thin coats

Apply the first coat with a fine artist brush. Fill the chip flush with the enamel surface. Do not over-fill; the repair will sit proud and read as a bump. Allow each coat to dry to a light touch, then add a second coat if the first sank into the chip. Two thin coats are stronger than one thick coat.
Step 6, cure for 24 hours

Leave the bath empty for 24 hours after the final coat. Cool, dry, dust-free conditions. Do not run hot water in the room during the cure. The paint reaches full hardness in three days; a cautious user waits a long bath before the first soak.
Variations

On an acrylic bath the same chip repair uses a separate acrylic repair paste; the bath enamel paint will not bond to acrylic. Acrylic bath repair kits are sold under brand names like Plast-Aid or Acryfix. The acrylic kit has a powder and a liquid to mix, not two pastes.
Care

A repaired chip lasts five to ten years. Avoid abrasive cleaners on the repair area. A repair stands hot water and ordinary shower gel use; bleach and limescale remover can soften the paint film over time.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The repair paint colour does not match the bath
attrs.cause: Bath enamel ages and yellows over decades; a fresh repair paint reads as bright white against the older surface.
attrs.fix: Add a single drop of artist's ivory acrylic to the mixed repair before brushing. Test on a card first. Match by eye against the bath in daylight.

attrs.symptom: The repair flakes off within a few months
attrs.cause: The chip was not fully dry when painted, or the surface was not keyed and degreased.
attrs.fix: Scrape the loose paint out, repeat the prep steps in full, and rebuild in thin coats.
